01 Pure SVG — The Canvas You're Painting On

← Back to lessons

No JavaScript in this lesson. Just HTML. Every D3 chart is made of SVG elements. Before we touch D3, you need to be fluent in what it produces.

What is SVG?

SVG stands for Scalable Vector Graphics. It's a set of HTML tags for drawing shapes. You already know <div>, <p>, <img>. SVG adds tags like <rect>, <circle>, <line>, <text>.

An SVG element is just a container, like a <div>. You put shapes inside it:

<svg width="300" height="100">
  <rect x="10" y="10" width="200" height="80" fill="orange" />
</svg>

Result:

This is the entire foundation of D3. Every bar chart, line chart, map, and visualization that D3 produces is just SVG elements like this. D3 doesn't use canvas or WebGL by default — it writes these exact HTML tags into the page.

The Coordinate System (The One Gotcha)

SVG's coordinate system has one critical difference from what you'd expect:

  • (0, 0) is the top-left corner
  • X increases going right (normal)
  • Y increases going down (opposite of math class)

The coordinate system

Why this matters: When you make a bar chart, taller bars need smaller Y values (to reach higher on screen). This feels backwards at first, but you'll get used to it. D3's scales handle this for you later — but you need to know WHY.

Every Shape You Need to Know

<rect> — Rectangles (bars in bar charts)

<rect x="10" y="20" width="150" height="60" fill="#f97316" rx="4" />
<!--   x,y = top-left corner position
       width, height = size
       fill = color
       rx = corner radius (optional, makes it rounded) -->

Rectangles

Play with it: drag the sliders to move & resize a rect

<circle> — Circles (dots in scatter plots)

<circle cx="100" cy="60" r="40" fill="#818cf8" />
<!--   cx, cy = CENTER position (not top-left!)
       r = radius
       fill = color -->

Circles

Play with it: rect vs circle positioning

Notice how the rect moves from its top-left corner but the circle moves from its center. Drag the sliders — the crosshair marks the x/y or cx/cy point.

Notice: Circles use cx/cy (center x, center y), not x/y. This trips people up. Rects position from their top-left corner. Circles position from their center.

<line> — Lines

<line x1="10" y1="80" x2="200" y2="20" stroke="#4ade80" stroke-width="2" />
<!--   x1,y1 = start point
       x2,y2 = end point
       stroke = line color (not fill!)
       stroke-width = thickness -->

Lines

<text> — Labels

<text> is the only way to put words in SVG. It's weird in one way: the x, y position refers to where the text sits on its baseline (like where a letter rests on a ruled line), not the top-left corner like <rect>.

<text x="50" y="40" fill="#ccc" font-size="16">Hello SVG</text>
<!--   x,y = baseline position (think: where the letter sits on the line)
       fill = text color (not "color" like CSS!)
       font-size = just like CSS -->

Where does text actually sit?

The killer feature is text-anchor — it controls horizontal alignment. Think CSS text-align, but relative to the x-position you set:

text-anchor = how text aligns to its x position

text-anchor in action: labeling bars

Here's the real payoff. Look at this bar chart — every label uses text-anchor to align perfectly:

text-anchor="middle" — centering labels on bars

Notice: the bar starts at x="40" and is width="80", so its center is 40 + 80/2 = 80. Set the text's x="80" with text-anchor="middle" and it lines up perfectly. No guessing.

text-anchor in action: axis labels

Horizontal bar charts need right-aligned labels so they don't overlap the bars. That's text-anchor="end":

text-anchor="end" — right-aligning axis labels

Every state name has the same x="105", but text-anchor="end" makes them right-align to that point. The bars all start at x="115", leaving a clean 10px gap. Without text-anchor="end", "California" and "NY" would start at the same point and the short names would float in space.

The pattern: text-anchor="middle" for labels centered on bars (vertical charts). text-anchor="end" for labels beside bars (horizontal charts). You'll use one of these two in literally every chart you build.

<g> + transform — "Move everything as a group"

Here's the problem <g> solves. Say you draw a labeled bar — a rect + two text labels:

Without groups: manually position each shape
<rect x="100" y="50" ... />
<text x="100" y="45" ...>39.5M</text>
<text x="140" y="90" ...>CA</text>

<!-- Now move it 200px right?
     Change x on ALL THREE elements.
     Tedious and error-prone. -->
With a group: move the container, everything follows
<g transform="translate(100, 50)">
  <rect x="0" y="0" ... />
  <text x="0" y="-5" ...>39.5M</text>
  <text x="40" y="40" ...>CA</text>
</g>

<!-- Move it? Just change translate().
     All children move together. -->

transform="translate(x, y)" means "slide this group x pixels right and y pixels down." Everything inside the group draws from (0, 0) of the group's new position. It's like position: relative in CSS.

Same bar + label, placed in 3 different positions just by changing translate()

Real-world use: In D3, you rarely position each shape individually. Instead, D3 creates a <g> for each data point, translates it into position, then draws the shapes inside at (0, 0). This is how axes work too — each tick mark is a <g> translated to the right spot.

The Margins Convention (why groups matter most)

Every D3 chart uses this pattern. The outer SVG is the full canvas. A single <g> group is translated inward to create margins for axes and labels:

The margins convention — a picture frame for your chart

This is the #1 D3 pattern. Every chart you build will start with: create SVG → define margins → create a <g> group translated by the margins. All chart code draws inside that group. Axes sit in the margin space around it. You'll see this in every lesson from here on.

<path> — Complex Shapes (lines, areas, arcs)

The <path> element draws anything using a mini drawing language in its d attribute. You almost never write path commands by hand — D3's generators write them for you. But you should know what they look like:

<path d="M 10 80 L 50 20 L 90 60 L 130 10 L 170 50"
      fill="none" stroke="#f97316" stroke-width="2" />
<!-- M = Move to   (pick up the pen)
     L = Line to   (draw a line)
     This draws a zig-zag line chart -->

Path: a line chart is just connected points

Key insight: A line chart in D3 is a single <path> element. D3's d3.line() generator takes your data array and writes the d="M ... L ... L ..." string for you. You never write path commands manually.

Quick Reference

ElementKey AttributesUsed For
<rect>x, y, width, height, fill, rxBars, backgrounds, heatmap cells
<circle>cx, cy, r, fillScatter plot dots, bubble charts
<line>x1, y1, x2, y2, strokeGrid lines, reference lines
<text>x, y, text-anchor, fill, font-sizeLabels, axis ticks, annotations
<path>d, fill, strokeLines, areas, arcs, map outlines
<g>transformGrouping, positioning, margins

fill vs stroke

  • fill = the inside color. Set fill="none" for just an outline.
  • stroke = the border/line color. Lines and paths NEED stroke to be visible.
  • opacity = transparency (0 = invisible, 1 = solid)

Build: A Bar Chart in Pure HTML

No JavaScript. Just type out the SVG. Here's a bar chart of the top 5 US states by population:

<svg width="600" height="280" style="background: #1a1a1a">
  <!-- California: 39.5M -->
  <text x="100" y="35" text-anchor="end" fill="#888">California</text>
  <rect x="110" y="15" width="395" height="30" fill="#f97316" rx="3" />
  <text x="515" y="35" fill="#666">39.5M</text>

  <!-- Texas: 29.1M -->
  <text x="100" y="85" text-anchor="end" fill="#888">Texas</text>
  <rect x="110" y="65" width="291" height="30" fill="#f97316" rx="3" />
  <text x="411" y="85" fill="#666">29.1M</text>

  <!-- ... and so on -->
</svg>

Result: hand-coded bar chart (zero JavaScript)

That's a bar chart. Five rectangles and ten text labels. No framework, no library, no build step. This is what D3 produces. When you see complex D3 code, remember: the end result is always just shapes like these.

Build: A Scatter Plot in Pure HTML

A scatter plot is just circles positioned by two data values. Here's one showing median income vs. voter turnout by state (made up but realistic data):

Hand-coded scatter plot

A scatter plot is <circle> elements. Each circle's cx represents one variable (income), cy represents another (turnout). That's the entire concept.

The Inspect Trick

Here's something powerful: right-click any D3 visualization on the web and click "Inspect". You'll see the raw SVG elements — the same <rect>, <circle>, <path>, <text> tags you just learned. Try it on the charts above.

Do this now: Right-click the bar chart above, hit "Inspect Element." You'll see exactly the HTML we wrote. There's no magic layer between D3's code and what appears on screen. This is why SVG fluency matters — when you debug D3, you're debugging SVG.

Check Your Understanding

1. Where is (0, 0) in SVG, and which direction does Y increase?

Top-left corner. Y increases going DOWN. This is the opposite of math coordinates and it's why chart-building requires flipping values.

2. What's the difference between how <rect> and <circle> are positioned?

<rect> uses x, y for its top-left corner. <circle> uses cx, cy for its center point. This is a common source of off-by-one positioning bugs.

3. A line is invisible even though you set x1, y1, x2, y2. What did you forget?

stroke. Lines don't use fill — they need a stroke color to be visible. You probably also want stroke-width.

4. What does <g transform="translate(50, 30)"> do?

It creates a group and shifts everything inside it 50 pixels right and 30 pixels down. All child elements use (50, 30) as their new origin (0, 0). D3 uses this for the margins convention.

5. What SVG element does D3 use to draw a line chart?

A single <path> element. D3's line generator writes the d attribute string (the "M ... L ... L ..." commands) from your data array. The path connects all the points into one continuous line.

6. You want to label a bar chart's bars with centered text. What attribute centers text horizontally?

text-anchor="middle". Set the text's x to the center point you want, and text-anchor="middle" makes the text center itself on that point.

7. What's the difference between fill and stroke?

fill is the inside color. stroke is the outline/border color. Rectangles and circles default to black fill. Lines and paths have no fill by default and need stroke to be visible.

Exercise: Hand-Code These Charts

Open code/exercises/01-exercise.html in your editor. No JavaScript — just write SVG by hand:

  1. Vertical bar chart: 4 bars going upward from a baseline. Remember: taller bars need smaller Y values.
  2. Add labels: State name below each bar, value above each bar (use text-anchor="middle").
  3. Add color: Make each bar a different color.
  4. Bonus: Add a horizontal line at y=some value as a "national average" reference line with a label.

This forces you to think in SVG coordinates. Once this feels natural, D3 will make way more sense.

Recap

  1. SVG is just HTML — tags for shapes instead of text content
  2. Y is upside down — (0,0) is top-left, Y increases downward
  3. 6 elements cover 95% of D3 charts: rect, circle, line, text, path, g
  4. rect positions from top-left, circle positions from center
  5. fill = inside color, stroke = outline color
  6. <g> + translate = the foundation of D3's margins and layout
  7. <path> draws complex shapes — D3 generates the d string for you
  8. Inspect element on any D3 chart reveals these same tags
← Home Lesson 02: Scales & Axes →
